raw_case:
Spontaneous Pneumomediastinum in a Patient With Undiagnosed Ankylosing Spondylitis
Spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) is an uncommon condition characterized by air in the interstices of the mediastinum. Management generally involves supportive care; however, if a patient inspires high concentrations of oxygen, the mediastinal air will be absorbed faster. A 23-year-old man who presented with acute-onset breathlessness with a history of more than a year of lower backache was diagnosed with SPM and accompanying ankylosing spondylitis (AS) by a chest CT and spinal MRI and was treated conservatively. This case is being reported for its uniqueness, as SPM with underlying AS is rare.

Introduction

Pneumomediastinum is defined as the presence of air in the mediastinum. It is classified into two categories: spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) and secondary pneumomediastinum [1]. The term “spontaneous pneumomediastinum” is used when there is no obvious antecedent cause (such as surgery, infection, trauma, or viscus perforation) in otherwise healthy individuals. The condition is often misdiagnosed due to the vague nature of its presentation. SPM must be included in the differential diagnosis of chest pain to enable prompt management [2]. Chest discomfort and dyspnea, two of the most common presenting symptoms of SPM, can be caused by a variety of cardiopulmonary illnesses, making the diagnosis of SPM challenging. Despite the rarity of SPM, early identification and treatment are critical to prevent worsening of the condition.

Case presentation

A 23-year-old man with a history of lower backache of more than one year presented to our emergency department with acute-onset breathlessness beginning one day before. His blood pressure was 120/80 mmHg, temperature was 97.9ºF, respiratory rate was 30 cycles/min, pulse rate was 134 BPM, and oxygen saturation was 88% on room air. The patient was tachypneic, and subcutaneous crepitus was felt on examination. On auscultation, crackles were heard in the bilateral interscapular and mammary regions, accompanied by a pericardial rub. His laboratory values were remarkable, with a total leukocyte count of 14,000/dL. A chest X-ray revealed pneumomediastinum (Figure 1), and a chest CT was remarkable for bilateral subcutaneous emphysema and moderate pneumomediastinum (with pneumopericardium), with air tracking along the bilateral lung fields (predominantly along bilateral oblique fissures) and patchy areas of ground-glass opacities in the left upper lobe and anteromedial segment of the left lower lobe (Figure 2).An echocardiogram was performed, which was normal. The patient was managed with IV antibiotics for five days, along with oxygen support at 10 L/min for the first two days, after which his oxygen requirement decreased and his symptoms improved. In light of the patient’s history of more than one year of persistent lower backache with morning stiffness, a pelvic X-ray (Figure 3) and spinal MRI (Figure 4) were conducted, which revealed bilateral sacroiliitis. In light of the MRI findings, a test for HLA-B27 was ordered, which returned positive. The patient is currently being managed with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for lower backache. A repeat chest CT was performed, which showed a resolution of the patient’s pneumomediastinum (Figures 5, 6). 

Discussion

Pneumomediastinum (also known as mediastinal emphysema) is characterized by the presence of air in the mediastinum [2]. SPM is a rare condition with a male-to-female (M:F) ratio of 8:13 and is typically linked to underlying respiratory conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and bronchial asthma [3]. SPM usually presents with chest discomfort and dyspnea. Other symptoms include cough, dysphagia, a nasal voice, dysphonia, and cervical pain [4]. In this case, dyspnea was the only presenting symptom. In terms of diagnostic investigation, a chest CT picks up pneumomediastinum in most cases if an ordered chest X-ray is inconclusive. In the present case, a chest X-ray showed features of pneumomediastinum that were confirmed by chest CT. Along with features of pneumomediastinum, patchy areas of ground glass opacities were observed in a few segments of the left lung. The patient did not have any underlying respiratory symptoms prior to this acute event. Serious illnesses, such as pulmonary embolism, acute coronary syndrome, primary pneumothorax, and pericarditis, are included in the differential diagnosis of SPM. Upper lobe fibrosis, spontaneous pneumothorax, sleep apnea, ventilatory dysfunction due to chest wall restriction, and interstitial lung disease are other pulmonary manifestations of the disease [5]. With the enhanced lung parenchyma visibility made possible by high-resolution CT, interstitial lung disease is now a recognized feature of lung involvement in AS, in addition to apical fibrosis. These conditions were ruled out in our patient with a chest CT and 2D echocardiography. The patient responded to supportive management with oxygen therapy, which eventually led to a complete resolution of symptoms, as is observed in most cases. The patient was further evaluated for inflammatory back pain with a spinal MRI, which revealed features of sacroiliitis. A subsequent HLA B-27 test was found to be positive. The patient was thus diagnosed with ankylosing spondylitis (AS).

Conclusions

In this case report, we present a rare instance of SPM in a patient with AS. The development of pneumomediastinum in AS patients is unusual and warrants attention due to the potential complications. The patient’s presentation with chest pain and dyspnea led to the diagnosis, confirmed by imaging studies, which revealed air in the mediastinum without any apparent traumatic or iatrogenic cause. Conservative management was successful, with the patient recovering without any surgical intervention.This case highlights the importance of considering pneumomediastinum in the differential diagnosis of chest pain in AS patients. Early recognition and appropriate management are crucial to prevent complications. Further studies are needed to understand the association between AS and SPM and to develop guidelines for its management in this patient population.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 23-year-old male
- Chief Complaint: Acute-onset breathlessness
- History of Present Illness: Acute breathlessness starting one day prior to presentation; history of lower backache with morning stiffness lasting more than one year.
- Past Medical History: None reported
- Personal History: None mentioned
- Family History: None mentioned
- Physical Examination: Tachypnea (respiratory rate 30 cycles/min), oxygen saturation 88% on room air, subcutaneous crepitus on palpation, bilateral crackles on auscultation, pericardial rub.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Chest X-ray and CT: Confirmed moderate pneumomediastinum, bilateral subcutaneous emphysema, pneumopericardium, air tracking in lung fields, and ground-glass opacities in specific lung areas.
  - Echocardiogram: Normal findings.
  - Pelvic X-ray and spinal MRI: Bilateral sacroiliitis.
  - HLA-B27 test: Positive.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Pneumomediastinum (SPM):** Confirmed by chest imaging showing air in the mediastinum without obvious traumatic or infectious causes and with typical symptoms such as dyspnea and chest discomfort. Ruled out secondary pneumomediastinum due to lack of evident antecedent causes such as trauma or infection. 
   
2. **Pulmonary embolism/acute coronary syndrome/pericarditis:** Excluded by imaging (chest CT, 2D echocardiogram) and lack of clinical signs specific to these conditions (e.g., normal findings on echocardiogram for cardiac causes).

3. **Primary pneumothorax:** Differentiated from SPM via chest CT findings showing no evidence of pneumothorax but confirmation of pneumomediastinum.

4. **Underlying respiratory conditions (e.g., asthma, COPD):** No prior history, symptoms, or imaging findings supporting these conditions.

5. **Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS):** Considered due to chronic lower backache with morning stiffness. Confirmed by spinal MRI findings of sacroiliitis and positive HLA-B27 test.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
- **Spontaneous Pneumomediastinum (SPM):** Based on characteristic imaging findings, absence of trauma or infections, and successful response to supportive care (oxygen therapy).
- **Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS):** Diagnosed due to chronic inflammatory back pain with sacroiliitis on MRI and positive HLA-B27 status.

results.content:
### Resoning:

First, I need to approach this case systematically. The patient is a 23-year-old male presenting with acute-onset breathlessness that started one day prior to presentation. He also has a history of lower backache with morning stiffness lasting more than a year. Let's break down the information step by step.

Starting with the chief complaint of acute breathlessness, I need to consider possible causes of sudden onset dyspnea. Potential causes could include pulmonary embolism, pneumothorax, acute exacerbation of asthma or COPD, heart failure, or even anxiety. However, given the presence of other findings, I need to consider less common but serious conditions.

Looking at the physical examination, the patient has tachypnea with a respiratory rate of 30 cycles per minute and an oxygen saturation of 88% on room air, indicating hypoxemia. Subcutaneous crepitus is noted on palpation, which suggests the presence of air in the subcutaneous tissues. Bilateral crackles on auscultation could indicate pulmonary edema or inflammation, while a pericardial rub suggests pericardial irritation or inflammation.

Ancillary tests provide more specific information. The chest X-ray and CT confirm moderate pneumomediastinum, bilateral subcutaneous emphysema, pneumopericardium, air tracking in lung fields, and ground-glass opacities in specific lung areas. These findings are suggestive of alveolar rupture leading to air leakage into surrounding tissues, which could be due to several causes including barotrauma, coughing, vomiting, or more importantly, in this context, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) syndrome or other causes of acute hemorrhagic pneumonia.

However, the echocardiogram is normal, which makes conditions like pulmonary embolism or cardiac causes less likely, although not entirely ruled out. The pelvic X-ray and spinal MRI show bilateral sacroiliitis, which is inflammation of the sacroiliac joints, commonly seen in inflammatory arthritis conditions such as ankylosing spondylitis.

The HLA-B27 test is positive, which is a genetic marker often associated with certain autoimmune and inflammatory disorders, particularly ankylosing spondylitis and related spondyloarthropathies.

Now, connecting these dots, the patient has acute breathlessness with evidence of pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium, along with bilateral ground-glass opacities on imaging. Additionally, he has a history of lower backache and morning stiffness, bilateral sacroiliitis on imaging, and a positive HLA-B27 test.

Given these findings, I need to consider a condition that can cause both the respiratory symptoms and the musculoskeletal issues. One condition that comes to mind is Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS), which is a chronic inflammatory arthritis that primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints and is strongly associated with HLA-B27. However, AS typically doesn't present with acute respiratory symptoms like pneumomediastinum and pneumopericardium.

Another condition to consider is Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) syndrome, which is a multisystem inflammatory disorder that affects the eyes, meninges, ears, and skin, and is also associated with HLA-B27 in some populations. VKH can present with uveitis, meningitis, hearing loss, and dermatological manifestations. However, respiratory involvement in VKH is not typical, although pulmonary involvement has been reported in some cases.

Given the presence of pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium, I need to consider other causes such as barotrauma, cough-induced rupture of alveoli (Boerhaave syndrome is a tear in the esophagus, but that doesn't fit here), or inflammatory processes leading to alveolar damage.

Considering the patient's history of backache and stiffness, along with sacroiliitis and HLA-B27 positivity, it seems that he has an underlying spondyloarthritis, likely ankylosing spondylitis. However, the acute respiratory symptoms seem more severe and acute than what is typically seen in AS.

I need to think about whether the respiratory symptoms could be related to the underlying inflammatory condition. Inflammatory arthritis can sometimes be associated with respiratory manifestations, such as fibrosing alveolitis or other interstitial lung diseases, but these are usually chronic processes, not acute presentations.

Alternatively, the respiratory symptoms could be coincidental and unrelated to the underlying spondyloarthritis. For example, the patient could have had an episode of forceful coughing or vomiting that led to alveolar rupture and subsequent pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema.

However, the presence of ground-glass opacities on imaging suggests there is parenchymal lung involvement, which might indicate an infectious or inflammatory process.

Given that the patient is hypoxic and has multiple signs of air leak syndrome, immediate management would focus on supportive care, including oxygen therapy to correct hypoxemia, and possibly monitoring in an intensive care unit.

In terms of diagnosis, I need to consider conditions that can cause both the respiratory symptoms and the musculoskeletal issues, keeping in mind the HLA-B27 positivity.

One condition that comes to mind is diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (DAH), which can present with acute respiratory distress and ground-glass opacities on imaging. DAH can be associated with various autoimmune diseases, including those related to HLA-B27, such as vasculitis or inflammatory arthritis.

Another possibility is Goodpasture syndrome, which involves anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies leading to pulmonary hemorrhage and glomerulonephritis. However, this is typically associated with specific antibody testing, which isn't mentioned here.

Infectious causes should also be considered, such as bacterial pneumonia or fungal infections, although the imaging findings don't specifically point to a typical infectious process.

Given the complexity of the patient's presentation, it's possible that he is experiencing an acute exacerbation of his underlying spondyloarthritis with respiratory involvement, possibly involving the lungs directly.

Alternatively, the respiratory symptoms could be due to a separate process that has coincided with his underlying condition.

In summary, the patient has acute breathlessness with evidence of pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumopericardium, and ground-glass opacities on imaging, along with a history of lower backache, sacroiliitis, and HLA-B27 positivity.

### Answer:

The final diagnosis is Ankylosing Spondylitis with acute respiratory complication, possibly due to alveolar rupture and air leak syndrome.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) was confirmed by chest imaging showing air in the mediastinum without obvious traumatic or infectious causes and with typical symptoms such as dyspnea and chest discomfort.
<Step 2> Secondary pneumomediastinum was ruled out due to the lack of evident antecedent causes such as trauma or infection.

<Step 3> Pulmonary embolism, acute coronary syndrome, and pericarditis were excluded based on imaging findings (chest CT, 2D echocardiogram) and the absence of clinical signs specific to these conditions.
<Step 4> Primary pneumothorax was differentiated from SPM via chest CT findings, which showed no evidence of pneumothorax but confirmed pneumomediastinum.
<Step 5> Underlying respiratory conditions such as asthma and COPD were excluded due to the absence of prior history, symptoms, or imaging findings supporting these conditions.
<Step 6> Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) was considered due to chronic lower backache with morning stiffness and confirmed by spinal MRI findings of sacroiliitis and a positive HLA-B27 test.

<Step 7> The final diagnosis of spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) was made based on characteristic imaging findings, absence of trauma or infections, and successful response to supportive care such as oxygen therapy.
<Step 8> The final diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis (AS) was made based on chronic inflammatory back pain, sacroiliitis on MRI, and positive HLA-B27 status.
